rationales.self_direction: He pushes back on the family's prescribed path, saying their only version of success "has a degree attached to it" while insisting he's "building something."
rationales.achievement: He points to concrete business wins, including "closed a deal with two new restaurants this week," as evidence of real success.
rationales.power: He wants others to recognize that he's not "just some kid running errands" but someone running a growing operation, showing a strong need for status and recognition.
He fixates on being "the one who fixes everything" and wonders if he "can't stand not being in the middle of it," showing attachment to control.
rationales.benevolence: He let Sami take Friday off "for a family thing" and absorbed the burden himself by doing the route.

rationales.achievement: Despite being underestimated, he "got the deal in the end because my prices were better," showing competitive competence and results-focus.
rationales.power: What bothered him most was not just the money but that he wasn't treated as "the one making decisions," showing a strong need for authority and status.

rationales.hedonism: He turned down football even though "the routes were fine," choosing oversight over leisure and present enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: He takes pride in a high-performing day where "all three scooters" were running smoothly and both riders "hit their targets."
rationales.power: Paying bonuses and wanting to be "right there watching it happen" reflects satisfaction in directing others and staying at the center of operations.
rationales.security: He says he doesn't "trust good days to last if I'm not paying attention," showing active vigilance to preserve stability.
rationales.benevolence: He rewarded Sami and Yousef with "a small bonus this week," using his success to support people working for him.

rationales.power: He hates feeling reduced to "just his son moving boxes" and wants to be seen as "the guy managing a fleet," which centers status and standing.
rationales.benevolence: Even while resentful, he agreed to help because "his back is bad again," responding to his father's need.

rationales.achievement: He frames the loss in terms of what he built, from hiring and training Yousef to the business impact of "losing a route" and income.
rationales.power: He takes the departure personally because "what I built wasn't worth staying for," showing that loyalty to his enterprise and position matters deeply to him.

rationales.stimulation: He dismisses the safer option because "reliable is boring" and prefers the candidate who is "hungrier," showing attraction to intensity and risk.
rationales.achievement: He wants workers who will "hustle" and "prove something," reflecting a strong performance-and-results orientation.
rationales.power: He is actively selecting and shaping his team, already "planning his routes" and looking for people who understand "what we're building."
rationales.security: He knowingly rejects the stable choice Khaled recommends and treats reliability as less appealing than drive, which cuts against prioritizing predictability.

rationales.hedonism: He spent the day covering "thirteen deliveries in the rain" until his "hands are still shaking from the cold," a clear sacrifice of comfort and pleasure.
rationales.power: His reflection that maybe Nader "didn't want to answer to someone else" shows how central authority and control are in how he interprets the failure.

rationales.self_direction: He recoils from a life like Ahmad's, saying he'd "rather eat glass" than ask permission before going anywhere, which strongly favors autonomy.
rationales.power: He explicitly links his identity to being "in charge" and to people "respecting me," showing how much control and standing matter to him.
rationales.conformity: He rejects the expected path to "find a girl and settle down" and openly despises the idea of asking family permission to live his life.

rationales.achievement: He notes concrete growth, telling his father he now works with "eleven" restaurants and reflecting on the business progress with pride.
rationales.security: He comes to value Issa being "reliable" and appreciates that the routes were handled without his intervention, signaling comfort with steadier, more dependable systems.

rationales.self_direction: She relishes that the brief gave her "so much room to play" and defends choosing a life that includes pleasure on her own terms.
rationales.hedonism: She savors the morning at Leopold's, orders "three cups of chai," and argues that "you should actually enjoy" what you're hustling for.
rationales.achievement: She stayed productive, saying "the sketches are coming together" and "I finished the commission brief" while balancing enjoyment with work.

rationales.self_direction: She insists "I KNOW this is what I'm meant to do" and refuses to treat salary or marriage as the only valid future.
rationales.conformity: The whole conflict is her refusal to accept the expected path of "salary, benefits, no husband" and her exhaustion at having to defend her choices.
rationales.benevolence: When she's upset, she leans on close support as "Rohan came over with biriyani and let me rant for an hour," highlighting the importance of intimate care and understanding.

rationales.hedonism: After a good teaching day she rewards herself with "fancy dark chocolate on the way home because why not," embracing simple present pleasure.
rationales.benevolence: She invests energy in the kids' creativity, especially noticing and celebrating Advait's collage rather than treating the class as routine work.

rationales.achievement: She completed the mural, the "owner loved it," and there is already "repeat interest," all of which point to successful, recognized performance.

rationales.self_direction: She says she "deliberately chosen this one" after considering other paths, emphasizing independent authorship of her career.
rationales.conformity: She resists pressure to make her art "something practical" and is tired of being expected to align with safer social norms.

rationales.hedonism: She is "running on fumes" after three workshops and realizes she needs to protect her energy, showing current behavior has come at the expense of enjoyment and comfort.
rationales.achievement: Doing "three workshops back to back" for "good money" reflects sustained effort and output despite fatigue.

rationales.self_direction: She defines success on her own terms as "a studio of my own" and "the freedom to say no to projects that don't excite me."
rationales.hedonism: The entry celebrates rest, comfort, and pleasure, from sleeping in and listening to music to calling the Goa trip "Non-negotiable."
rationales.security: She wants "enough commissions to stay comfortable" and splits the mural payment between "rent and supplies," showing attention to practical stability.
rationales.conformity: She explicitly rejects "society's very narrow definition" of success and says it takes courage to "opt out of the rat race."

rationales.self_direction: She defines her life around what she personally wants, saying "this is exactly where I want to be" and rejecting accumulation as the goal.
rationales.stimulation: The trip is full of novelty and vivid experience, from Goa travel to "the best fish curry" and filling "half a sketchbook" with new scenes.
rationales.hedonism: "Zero regrets" and "doing things that feel good" make present enjoyment the explicit point of the trip.
rationales.conformity: She pushes back on conventional work-and-status priorities by saying "Not to accumulate stuff" and treating pleasure and relationships as the real goal.
rationales.benevolence: She centers "people I love" and says time with "the people who matter" is what makes the experience meaningful.
